literal: being, existing; present participle of √as 'to be'
invariant_definition: Being or reality, but in technical contexts more than the bare copula 'is'. *Sat* marks what stands up under ontological and causal analysis. The term therefore appears in several linked debates: whether only the ultimate is *sat* in the strict sense or whether dependent realities are also genuinely *sat*; whether effects arise from what already is, as *sat-kārya-vāda* argues, or from prior non-manifestation; and how *sat* differs from both *asat* and, in Advaita, *mithyā*. Upaniṣadic usage gives the term high metaphysical weight, especially in *sad eva somya idam agra āsīt*; later schools then ask what exactly counts as 'what is'. For Advaita, *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to *brahman*. For realist Vedāntas and Sāṃkhya, dependent entities are also real, though not equally independent. A translation that treats *sat* as mere existence misses this layered use in causality, ontology, and soteriology.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to the self that is *prapañcopaśama*, *śānta*, and *advaita*; what is ultimately real is that fourth beyond all transactional predication [[MāṇḍU 7](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7)] [[GK 2.32](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32)]. Avidyā and the world are therefore not granted unqualified *sattā*; Maṇḍana formulates avidyā as neither *satī* nor *asatī* and hence *anirvacanīyā* [[BS 1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. Later Advaita accordingly treats Brahman itself as *satyajñānasukhātmaka*, so that *sat* functions as a *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* of the non-dual absolute rather than as a predicate shared univocally by appearances [[Advaita-Siddhi 1](cite://madhusudana/advaita-siddhi/1)].

In Viśiṣṭādvaita, *sat* is one of the Vedāntic words whose principal referent is Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa, the *paratattva* who stands as the inner controller of all *cit* and *acit* [[Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Because the universe is taught through *sāmānādhikaraṇya* with that *śarīrin*, dependent realities are not expelled from being but ordered as his body and mode [[Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Vedānta Deśika can therefore gloss *sat* as what is *prāmāṇika* and *upādeya* for the *mumukṣu*, while *asat* is what fails that normative measure [[Sarvārtha-Siddhi ad 1.2b](cite://vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2b)] [[Sarvārtha-Siddhi ad 1.2c](cite://vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2c)].

In Tattva-vāda, *sat* is said without reservation of the *prapañca* articulated by *bhedapañcaka*; the fivefold difference is *satya* and beginningless [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Texts of apparent non-difference are therefore read through *sādṛśya* and dependence, never as *svarūpābhidā*; even in liberation the *jīva* retains qualities similar to the Lord's while remaining distinct [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. Madhva's anti-māyāvāda polemic accordingly concludes that, once the rival account fails, *satyatā* belongs to difference itself [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana p. 238](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/would instead be true)].

In Jīva Gosvāmī's *Sat-Sandarbha*, the *abheda-śāstras* are admissible only once Bhagavān's distinction from the *jīva* has been secured and the latter has been understood as a conscious dependent ray upheld by his *svābhāvika-tad-acintya-śakti* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Bhagavān is then fixed as the *parama-svarūpa* of all, as the sun in relation to its rays, so that ontological ultimacy remains personal and devotional rather than collapsing into featureless being [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. Once that bheda is settled, *bhajana* alone has *abhidheya*-status [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

For Śuddhādvaita, Brahman is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa* of the world because it pervades the effect as *asti bhāti priyatvena sac-cid-ānanda-rūpeṇa*; *sat* is thus Brahman's own pervasive form within manifestation [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. Names and forms are only *kārya-dharmas*, so the world is not downgraded to illusory appearance but retained as real expression of that same Brahman [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. This is why Vallabha can hold *avikṛta-pariṇāma*: Brahman truly becomes the effect while remaining unmodified in itself [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)].

Mādhyamaka handles *sat* through the discipline of the two truths: *lokasaṃvṛtisatya* and *paramārthasatya* are both indispensable to dharmadeśanā, but they are not two layers of self-standing being [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)]. To miss their *vibhāga* is precisely to miss *tattva* in the Buddha's teaching [[MMK 24.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.9)].

For this line, what is *paramārthasat* is whatever is *arthakriyā-samartha*; ultimate reality is marked by causal efficacy [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)]. What lacks that status is *saṃvṛtisat*, the level of conceptual and general construction, so the distinction between the real and the merely conventional is drawn within cognition's own economy [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.2](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.2)] [[Pramāṇavārttika 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)].

translator_note: Avoid treating *sat* as mere existential vocabulary. English 'being' and 'existence' encourage a thin, quantificational reading, whereas Sanskrit usage often marks robust reality, causal persistence, and resistance to sublation. Also keep *sat* distinct from *satya* ('true') and from the Advaita contrast-term *mithyā*.
